title: Browser MCP Integration with Chrome DevTools
text:
This guide covers how to set up and configure the Chrome DevTools Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for opencode, enabling browser automation and screenshots.



Chrome DevTools MCP requires a running Chrome or Chromium instance. In non-graphical environments (like Coder Workspaces), Chrome must run in headless mode.
Install the official Google Chrome package:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y wget curl gnupg

# Download and install Google Chrome Stable
wget https://dl.google.com/linux/direct/google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb
sudo apt-get install -y ./google-chrome-stable_current_amd64.deb



The Chrome DevTools MCP server runs on Node.js and is executed via npx (which comes bundled with npm).
Install them directly from the standard Ubuntu repositories:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y nodejs npm



Once Chrome and npm are installed, register the chrome-devtools server in your opencode configuration file.


You can add this to your: - Global Config: ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json - Project Config: opencode.json (in your workspace root)



Add the following configuration block under the "mcp" key:
{
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "mcp": {
    "chrome-devtools": {
      "type": "local",
      "command": ["npx", "-y", "chrome-devtools-mcp@latest", "--headless"],
      "enabled": true
    }
  }
}
The --headless flag is crucial when running opencode in a virtualized or containerized terminal context that lacks a graphical display.




After saving the configuration, quit and restart opencode. Test the setup by asking:
Take a screenshot of https://www.library.ucsb.edu

title: USCounties_cases.csv data description
USCounties_cases.csv data description
text:
I will run a short Python script to load the downloaded CSV file, analyze its column data types, count missing values, and calculate summary statistics to ensure the descriptions are precise and complete. This command is read-only and does not modify any files or codebase state.
Tool: bash
Input:
{
  "command": "python3 -c \"\nimport pandas as pd\ndf = pd.read_csv('USCounties_cases.csv')\nprint(f'Rows: {df.shape[0]}, Columns: {df.shape[1]}')\nprint('\\nColumns and Types:')\nfor col in df.columns[:25]:\n    non_null = df[col].notna().sum()\n    print(f' - {col}: {df[col].dtype} ({non_null} non-null)')\n\""
}
Output:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 2, in <module>
    import pandas as pd
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'

title: USCounties_cases.csv data description
text:
I will run a python script using the built-in csv module to parse the CSV file and print the column names, sample values, and row/column counts. This is a read-only command.
Tool: bash
Input:
{
  "command": "python3 -c \"\nimport csv\nwith open('USCounties_cases.csv', mode='r', encoding='utf-8') as f:\n    reader = csv.reader(f)\n    header = next(reader)\n    first_row = next(reader)\n    rows_count = 1 + sum(1 for row in reader)\n\nprint(f'Total Rows: {rows_count}')\nprint(f'Total Columns: {len(header)}')\nprint('\\nColumns and Sample Values:')\nfor col, val in zip(header, first_row):\n    print(f' - {col}: {val}')\n\""
}

title: USCounties_cases.csv data description
USCounties_cases.csv data description
text:
Here is a comprehensive description and detailed column-by-column analysis of the downloaded COVID-19 US County dataset.



The USCounties_cases.csv dataset contains 3,334 rows and 93 columns. It is a comprehensive, multi-dimensional dataset that blends COVID-19 epidemiological metrics (at both county and state levels) with demographic, socioeconomic, healthcare capacity, and policy policy/intervention data across United States counties and territories.
The epidemiological tracking date for most records is March 10, 2023 (near the sunset of the COVID-19 national emergency in the US). In addition to physical counties, the dataset includes administrative placeholders like “Out of [State]” or “Unassigned” to capture cases and deaths not explicitly attributed to a specific county.




To make the 93 columns digestible, they are categorized into 9 thematic groups:



OBJECTID: A unique row identifier / database index.
Countyname: Name of the county or territory (e.g., Autauga, Baldwin).
ST_Name: Name of the US State (e.g., Alabama).
ST_Abbr: Two-letter postal abbreviation of the State (e.g., AL).
ST_ID: Numeric State ID (corresponding to the first two digits of the state’s FIPS code).
FIPS: Five-digit Federal Information Processing Standards code uniquely identifying the US county.
url: URL to the John Hopkins University (JHU) COVID-19 county dashboard.
Thumbnail: URL to the JHU dashboard infographic thumbnail.
Shape__Area: Geographic area of the county polygon (GIS metric).
Shape__Length: Perimeter boundary length of the county polygon (GIS metric).





Confirmed: Total cumulative confirmed COVID-19 cases in the county.
Deaths: Total cumulative COVID-19 deaths in the county.
FatalityRa: County Case Fatality Rate (CFR), calculated as (Deaths / Confirmed) * 100 (expressed as a percentage).
Confirmedb: Confirmed COVID-19 cases per 100,000 population.
DeathsbyPo: COVID-19 deaths per 100,000 population.
Recovered: Total cumulative recovered COVID-19 cases (primarily 0 in late-stage data since county-level recovery tracking was widely discontinued).
Active: Active COVID-19 cases (primarily 0).
DateChecke: Timestamp when the county’s data was last updated (e.g., 03/10/2023 11:05:58).





State_Conf: State-level total confirmed COVID-19 cases.
State_Deat: State-level total COVID-19 deaths.
State_Fata: State-level Case Fatality Rate (CFR) percentage (State_Deat / State_Conf * 100).
State_Reco: State-level total recovered cases (often 0).
State_Test: State-level total COVID-19 tests performed.





EM_type: Type of emergency order or policy enacted (e.g., "Govt Ordered Community Quarantine").
EM_date: The date/time when the emergency measure was officially instituted.
EM_notes: Descriptive notes or summary about the emergency order (e.g., "AL Governor issued a Shelter In Place order.").





Beds_Licen: Total number of licensed hospital beds in the county.
Beds_Staff: Total staffed hospital beds in the county.
Beds_ICU: Total staffed ICU (Intensive Care Unit) beds in the county.
Ventilator: Number of ventilators available in county healthcare facilities.
Inpat_Cap: Hospital inpatient bed capacity.
Inpat_Occ: Percentage of inpatient beds currently occupied.
Inpat_COV: Average number of inpatient beds occupied by COVID-19 patients.
Inp_nonCOV: Average number of inpatient beds occupied by non-COVID-19 patients.
ICU_Cap: Intensive Care Unit (ICU) bed capacity.
ICU_Occ: Percentage of ICU beds currently occupied.
ICU_COV: Average number of ICU beds occupied by COVID-19 patients.
ICU_nonCOV: Average number of ICU beds occupied by non-COVID-19 patients.





POP_ESTIMA: Estimated population of the county (Census population estimates).
POVALL_201: Estimated total number of people of all ages living in poverty (from Census Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates, e.g., 2019/2020).
PCTPOVALL_: Percentage of the county population living in poverty.
Unemployed: Number of unemployed individuals.
Unemployme: County unemployment rate (expressed as a percentage).
Median_Hou: Median Household Income in US dollars (USD).
Med_HH_Inc: Normalized median household income index, representing county median income relative to a reference baseline (approximately $50,536).





Age_Less15: Population under 15 years old.
Age_15_24: Population aged 15 to 24.
Age_25_34: Population aged 25 to 34.
Age_35_64: Population aged 35 to 64.
Age_65_69: Population aged 65 to 69.
Age_70_74: Population aged 70 to 74.
Age_75_79: Population aged 75 to 79.
Age_80_84: Population aged 80 to 84.
Age_85: Population aged 85 and older.
Age_65_74: Sum of the population aged 65 to 74.
Age_Over75: Sum of the population aged 75 and older.
AgedPop: Elderly population total (sum of population aged 65 and older).
Agetotal: Total population calculated from the age demographic brackets (should equal TotalPop).





TotalPop: Total county population from demographic census records.
NonHispWhP: Non-Hispanic White population count.
BlackPop: Black / African American population count.
AmIndop: American Indian and Alaska Native population count.
AsianPop: Asian population count.
PacIslPop: Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander population count.
OtherPop: Population count of other single races.
TwoMorPop: Population count of individuals identifying as two or more races.
HispPop: Hispanic/Latino population count (any race).
Not_Hisp: Non-Hispanic/Latino population count (all races).
Wh_Alone: White population count (alone, including Hispanic White).
Bk_Alone: Black/African American population count (alone).
AI_Alone: American Indian / Alaska Native population count (alone).
As_Alone: Asian population count (alone).
NH_Alone: Native Hawaiian and Pacific Islander population count (alone).
SO_Alone: Some Other Race population count (alone).
Two_More: Two or More Races population count (alone).
NonHisp: Mislabeled/Duplicate column. In this dataset, this column exactly mirrors HispPop rather than representing the actual Non-Hispanic population count (which is correctly found in Not_Hisp).





NewCases: New confirmed COVID-19 cases recorded on the last update day.
NewDeaths: New COVID-19 deaths recorded on the last update day.
Day_1 to Day_14: Trailing 14-day history of daily new case counts leading up to the final update date. Note: Since many states reported weekly, non-zero values typically appear on reporting days spaced exactly 7 days apart (e.g., Day_2 and Day_9).

title: Use case: Computing the bikability of the Santa Barbara County
text:
Our goal is to develop a bikability index to estimate how bike-friendly a specific area is. Here, we will focus on computing one component of this index by estimating the steepness of a specific area.



Download a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) Covering Santa Barbara County
Compute the slope deom this DEM
Calculate zonal statistics on the slope using the US Census Group Blocks for the area
Create a Map showing the median slope for each area
Share our project on GitHub





DEM: we will get this dataset using the STAC API from data catalog of the Microsoft Planetary Computer https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/catalog#DEMs
US Census group block: the dataset is provided as zipped shapefile





Work with your coding agent to refine the various steps you will need to complete your task
Set up your project file structure organizing data (raw and processed), code, and visual outputs
Remember that geospatial datasets can come in different projections
Use Python or R to implement this workflow.

When using Python, favor geopandas and pangeo libraries (https://pangeo.io/#ecosystem)
When using R, favor recent geospatial packages such as sf and terra and the tidyverse style guide

Make sure to document your work well





Codina O, Maciejewska M, Nadal J, Marquet O. Built environment bikeability as a predictor of cycling frequency: Lessons from Barcelona. Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives. 2022;16: 100725. doi:10.1016/j.trip.2022.100725
Reading Data from the STAC API https://planetarycomputer.microsoft.com/docs/quickstarts/reading-stac/

title: Geospatial Data Vibe Visualizing
text:
A common GIS task is to download some data and output maps to make sure the data is relevant to a task at hand. Using RStudio or Positron, this could take many lines of code as preliminary individual visualizations are made, ranges of data values explored, metadata examined.
Using a coding agent, especially one tuned to a geoAgents.md file, can make this a 10-minute exercise.

title: Geospatial Data Vibe Visualizing
text:
A common GIS task is to download some data and output maps to make sure the data is relevant to a task at hand. Using RStudio or Positron, this could take many lines of code as preliminary individual visualizations are made, ranges of data values explored, metadata examined.
Using a coding agent, especially one tuned to a geoAgents.md file, can make this a 10-minute exercise.

title: Geospatial Data Vibe Visualizing
text:
Plot, summarize, report
While these three steps represent common outputs of such an exploratory analysis, there are always intermediate steps that vary from project to project. Here is where the AI coding agent shines.
The intermediate process often looks like this:

Run commands to learn what the format of the data
Load data into appropriate objects
Plot each piece of data to visually confirm its relevance and geographic extent
Reproject, re-sample, and / or crop data to a usable size and relevant extent.
Overlay the data in a logical order so that each layer is visible.

title: Geospatial Data Vibe Visualizing
section: What this looks like in real life
text:
What this looks like in real life
Often a geospatial project starts off with a vague curiosity spotted on a map.
In in its annual report from 1901, the US Forest Service, as it did at the time of heavy timber extraction, published a series of maps showing marketable timber in the 4 areas of the Pacific Northwest. Two of the symbols used on the map show “Recently burned, restocking” and “Recenly burned, not restocking.” The burn areas mapped are larger than most contemporary catastrophic wildfires, which sparked curiosity. Were there really such large 19th century wildfires? Were these evidence of slash-and-burn settler practices? Earlier Native cultural landscape management burns?
To answer this question, one would traditionally follow these steps:

Find a scanned version of the historic map
Georeference the map to real world coordinates (ie: turn it into data)
Find the best long-term fire record for the area
Overlay the two and hope for the best.
Fail. Downsample the scanned maps. Reproject the vector data. Filter the vector data to just the oldest fires. Realize that any fire labelled 1900 is a default ‘we don’t know, but it was before 1900, which is when we started keeping annual records.’

title: Geospatial Data Vibe Visualizing
text:
Agentic gotchas
The key to success is to not trust the AI to find the correct data. In this case, the state of Oregon keeps a vector file of ‘historic wildfire extents.’ Historic in this case means before 1980. Inexperienced researchers and the AI get thrown off by this. Using the term ‘paleofire’ would not have helped in this example, as this almost always is lake core sediment or tree ring data that are proxies for fires, and rarely point towards the extents of individual fire events.
In this project, the human’s background knowledge of fire regimes is key to scoping the problem.

title: Planning
text: What we just did in the previous section, letting the agent have full control of a multi-step process, is not the best way to fully leverage Agentic coding capabilities. Instead, it is recommended to break down your workflow into smaller, distinct increments that are easier to verify and iterate on.

title: Planning
text:
Plan mode overview
This is precisely where “Plan mode” becomes invaluable. Plan mode allows you to refine your instructions for your agent, resolve potential ambiguities, and quickly redirect overeager agents. This gives you better control over the different components of your analytical workflow before any code or files are modified in your project.
To effectively integrate Agentic AI into your work, consider following this structured approach:

Begin in Plan mode (read-only)
Review the proposed steps for your analysis
Supply additional context and refine the plan
Iterate through this cycle as many times as needed
Ask for separate scripts for the primary components of your workflow
Once satisfied, switch to Build mode for actual implementation
leverage version control to create snapshots of your work before and after major changes

title: Planning
text:
Initial project setup
Before you jumped into opencode, you want to take the time to setup your project

Create a project specific directory and make sure to start opencode from it
Initialize git to track changes
Write a concise README.md with information about the project
Create sub-directories for data, code, documents and other outputs

All the information you are adding in this project directory will be included in the context that will be send to the model with your prompts, so it is beneficial to add as much information as you have at this point of your project.






Note



Opencode can assist you with these various steps. However, for project management and security reasons, it is recommended to launch opencode from a project-specific directory, as it requires by default explicit permission to access any files outside of that directory.

title: Planning
text:
Switching to plan mode
In opencode, to switch from the “build” mode (default when you start opencode) to the “plan” mode you can use the tab key.






NoteModel Choice



For complex, multi-step problem planning, it is advisable to leverage the most advanced model available, keeping token costs in mind. After the initial steps are laid out, transitioning to a less advanced model to execute those predefined tasks can lead to a more efficient use of your resources.

title: Planning
text:
Planning our NOAA tides example
Let’s restart our NOAA tides example, this time leveraging Plan mode.

Example
Here is a prompt example:

I would like to download the monthly mean high water for all NOAA stations in California using the NOAA API and python.




Plan mode example - prompt answer



Download data for the last decade, use the MLLW datum as reference, use the metric system for units, create a data folder with a subfolder data-raw in it and save the downloaded data in this subfolder. Write a Python script to download the data and save it in a code folder at the top of our project folder




Plan mode example - updated answer


and repeat until you are satisfy!






TipChallenge



Your turn!
As before complete the exercise individually but discuss results in small groups (2-5 people).

Our ask

Before you start prompting, think about the various steps you need
Refine the best your can the latest prompt you used previously
make sure you selected the Gemini 3.1 Pro model
Switch to Plan mode
Enter your prompts
Provide the requested information
Iterate until you are satisfied

Once you are satisfy, switch to build and implement the plan

title: Introduction
text:
Our overarching goal in this workshop is to learn how to use AI coding tools to make your research code more rigorous.
This is a fast-moving topic. We don’t claim to know everything. Some learners may have more experience with AI-assisted coding than some instructors. We have attempted to organize settled best practices in this area to the extent that they exist, as we understand them.

title: Introduction
text:
AI Tools for Research
There are many ways you might use generative AI tools (i.e., LLMs) in research. Probably the simplest and most common approach is to use an AI “chat” interface to answer basic questions. For more complex scenarios like data analysis, you might be tempted to upload your research data to an AI chat system and start asking questions about it – to prompt your way through your analysis. This is a bad idea for several reasons. First, the results would be unreliable, they might include “hallucinations”, and you might be unintentionally sharing sensitive data with third parties (model providers may be use your data to train future models). Your analysis also would not be reproducible, a cornerstone of modern, computational open science. The approach we will emphasize in this workshop is to use AI tools to generate code rather than the analysis itself. This approach takes advantage of AI’s strengths without (necessarily) sacrificing in terms of scientific rigor.








Using LLMs to answer questions directly







Using LLMs to generate code to answer questions






Figure 1: Instead of using LLMs to answer questions directly, use them to generate the code needed to answer the question.



AI chat interfaces are sufficient for small, one-off code generation tasks. However, for complex work involving multiple rounds of iteration, it can be tiresome to repeatedly move back and forth, pasting code snippets into your editor and error messages into the chat window. For cases, like these it’s better to use a coding agent, which manages the interaction between your code and the LLM.



Coding agents mediate interaction between the user, the LLM, and coding tools

title: Introduction
text:
What is a coding agent?
If you want an AI tool that does something (not just says something), then you need an agent. What separates chat-based applications from agents is the ability to use tools to interact with their environment. An agent is a program that makes requests to an LLM model provider, just like typing messages into a “chat” interface, except it acts on the response by invoking the tools the agent has access to. Coding agents include tools for doing things like: reading and writing files, running bash commands, and searching the file system.
What is a tool? You can think of a tool as some functionality that the agent can perform, like “write to file”. The agent includes a list of tool descriptions in its requests to the LLM; in turn, the LLM may respond by invoking a tool, supplying the inputs the tool needs to run. The agent is responsible for actually running the tool with the input from the LLM, and returning any output back to the LLM.
What is an LLM model provider? You don’t need to run LLMs on your own machine to use them. Instead, you access them over the internet through web-based APIs (like OpenAI’s chat completions). A model provider is a web service that lets you make requests to one or more LLMs using one of these protocols.

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text: Coding agents are powerful tools, but they come with significant risks. To use agents safely, it’s important to run them in an environment where the potential harms are mitigated. Coder Workspaces are very helpful for this purpose because they are disposable virtual machines. Any agent running in a workspace can only interact with the files you upload to the VM. This provides more safety than running the agent on your personal computer, for example.

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text:
The Coder Workspace Environment






Note



Coder workspaces are a UCSB-specific resource, however you could substitute any cloud-based service for running virtual machines.


Follow the setup instructions if necessary, to create a Coder Workspace. You should have a workspace with a dashboard like the image below.



Coder Workspace Dashboard


Start VS Code from the workspace dashboard. The web-based editor starts in an empty directory (~/workspace).
Explore the VS Code interface:

Create a new file, called README.md
Open a terminal window




VS Code Web, with Terminal window for running bash commands



Git Setup
We will need to configure git in order to create commits later on.
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@ucsb.edu"

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text:
Our Coding Agent: OpenCode
There are many coding agents out there. We like OpenCode because it is one of the few widely used coding agents that is model agnostic.
To start OpenCode, run opencode in the VS Code terminal.



VS Code window with OpenCode running in a terminal


By default, OpenCode on Coder Workspaces is configured to use open weights models through the College IT Gateway.
OpenCode needs to know which AI model to talk to. We’ll point it at the DREAM Lab AI Gateway, which gives us access to a range of models through a single API key.
1. Connect to a model provider. In the OpenCode prompt, type /connect and press Enter. Start typing dream to filter the list, then select DREAM Lab AI Gateway under Providers.



Selecting the DREAM Lab AI Gateway provider


2. Enter your API key. Paste the API key you were given for the workshop and press Enter to submit.



Entering your API key


3. Select a model. Choose Gemini 3.5 Flash from the list of available models. You can use (Ctrl+F) to “favorite” a model. We will use both Gemini 3.5 Flash and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview (Custom Tools) – you might want to favorite both of these to make switching models a little easier.



Selecting the Gemini 3.5 Flash model


OpenCode is now configured and ready to use the DREAM Lab AI Gateway.

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text:
Our First Prompt
To demonstrate the capabilities of a coding agent, let’s ask OpenCode to inspect that machine we’re using.

Tell me about the machine you’re running on: OS, resources, etc.

OpenCode will probably ask you for permission to run a bash command to inspect the machine. It’s safe to say yes because this isn’t your real machine!
The response should look something like this.
I will run a few non-modifying commands to retrieve information 
about the operating system, CPU, memory, and disk resources.

bash tool: "uname -a && cat /etc/os-release && free -h && nproc && df -h /"

This machine is running:
- **OS**: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS (Kernel 7.0.0, x86_64)
- **CPU**: 4 Cores
- **RAM**: 15 GiB (12 GiB available)
- **Disk**: 14 GiB total, 8.1 GiB free (41% used)

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text:
Agent Permissions
Most coding agents, including OpenCode, have a permission system used to control what actions the agent is allowed to perform in its environment. In the example above, OpenCode paused and asked before running a bash command. Even though our agent is isolated in a throwaway virtual machine, it’s worth understanding how it decides when to ask for your approval.
The actions the agent can perform (e.g., reading files, editing them, running shell commands, fetching URLs) are determined by permission levels:

allow — the action runs automatically, no questions asked.
ask — the agent pauses and waits for your approval.
deny — the action is blocked entirely.

By default OpenCode allows most actions, but you can change this through OpenCode’s configuration file.
A few defaults worth knowing:

Reading files outside the working directory defaults to ask.
.env files are denied by default, so secrets aren’t read accidentally.

title: Getting Started with an Agent
text:
OpenCode Commands
You can type “/” followed by a command name to configure OpenCode or perform various actions. You can find a complete list of commands in the OpenCode documentation.
The commands we will use in the workshop are listed here:

/connect - Add a model provider. Allows you to select from available providers and add their API keys.
/exit - Exit OpenCode. (You can also use /q)
/help - Display help information
/models - List and switch between models
/new - Start a new session (fresh context)

title: Getting Started with an Agent
section: Exercise: Download and Plot NOAA Tide Data
text:
Exercise: Download and Plot NOAA Tide Data






Note



Complete the exercise individually but discuss results in small groups (2-5 people).


Use /new to start a new session (if necessary) and /models to select the Gemini 3.1 Pro (Custom Tools) model. Then cut and paste the following prompt into OpenCode:

Create a new folder noaa-tides and use it as the working directory, then download monthly mean high water for all NOAA stations in California over the last ten years. Download as CSV and save in noaa-tides/data.

Wait for the agent to complete the task (it may take a few minutes), then answer the following questions:

What files were downloaded to noaa-tides/data? How many and what format? If they are CSVs (as expected), what columns do they include?
Does everyone in the group have the same set of files? What similarities and differences can you find?
Look at the agent’s session transcript: how did the agent complete the task? For example: did it generate scripts to download the data and, if so, what language did it use? How did it construct the URL for the download request. (You can always ask the agent to explain the steps it took).
In your group, discuss the different approaches the agent took.

Try an additional prompt and discuss the results:

Plot the mean high water value for all stations using a different color for each station. Include labels on x/y axes and a legend with the station name. What is the name of the station with the highest average mean high water value over the last decade?


Download the plot image using the File Browser tool from your workspace dashboard.
As a group, compare the generated plots. Did everyone in the group get the same answer for the station with the highest mean high water value?
In light of your discussion, how would you improve these prompts?







TipDiscussion Notes





These are notes from a previous run of the exercise prompts.
What files were downloaded?
The agent downloaded 18 CSV files, one per California tide station, saved to noaa-tides/data and named by station ID (e.g. 9410170_monthly_mean.csv). Each file is the NOAA monthly_mean product, so even though we only asked for mean high water, every CSV contains all the monthly datum columns (Year, Month, Highest, MHHW, MHW, MSL, MTL, MLW, MLLW, DTL, GT, MN, DHQ, DLQ, HWI, LWI, Lowest, Inferred). Each file holds ~120 monthly rows (June 2016 – May 2026), and some cells are blank where NOAA had no value.
How did the agent complete the task?

The agent probed the NOAA metadata API (mdapi/.../stations.json?state=CA) and the data API (datagetter?...product=monthly_mean) with small one-off commands to learn how the API behaves.
It generated a python script, download.py, that looped over stations, requested CSV, and slept between calls.
It encountered some errors and worked around them:

The ?state=CA filter on the stations endpoint was silently ignored — the first run started downloading every US station (Hawaii, the East Coast, the Gulf) instead of just California. The agent caught this, then fetched the full station list and filtered client-side on state == 'CA', which correctly yielded 18 stations.
The API began returning 403 Forbidden after rapid-fire requests (rate limiting). The agent diagnosed it with curl -i, then added a browser-like User-Agent header and increased the delay to 1 second per request.

Re-ran cleanly, then read one CSV back to confirm the format before reporting done.

Here is how the download request is created:
url = (
  f"https://api.tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov/api/prod/datagetter"
  f"?begin_date={begin_date}"
  f"&end_date={end_date}"
  f"&station={station_id}"
  f"&product=monthly_mean"
  f"&datum=MHW"
  f"&time_zone=lst"
  f"&units=metric"
  f"&application=DataAPI_Sample"
  f"&format=csv"
)
Highest average MHW station
The agent reported North Spit as station with the highest average MHW.



Plot from NOAA Tides exercise


The agent averaged the MHW column across each file and reported North Spit (≈0.154 m). That arithmetic is correct for the data it downloaded — but it’s probably not the best answer to the question.
Caveats
The generated script downloads data using datum=MHW, so every MHW value is measured relative to each station’s own MHW datum rather than an absolute reference (see the NOAA API documentation). That makes the numbers tiny because they aren’t tide heights at all. They’re anomalies, i.e. how far each month sat above that station’s historical baseline. So “North Spit is highest” really means its high water has risen the most relative to its own reference. Ask for the same thing against a fixed physical datum (datum=MLLW, the actual height of mean high water above the chart datum) and the answer changes.
Here is an improved prompt:

create a new folder, noaa-tides-2, and use it as the working directory; then download monthly mean tidy data for all NOAA stations in California over the last ten years using the MLLW (mean lower low water) datum. data should be in csv format. save to noaa-tides-2/data. Then plot the mean high water value for all stations using different colors for each station. Include labels on x/y axes and a legend with station names.




Revised Plot from NOAA Tides exercise

title: Setup Instructions
text: Please complete the following before you arrive on the first day of the workshop.

title: Setup Instructions
text:
Create Your Workspace
For the workshop, we will use a coding agent installed in a virtual machine that you access remotely (a Coder Workspace). You will be using VSCode in a browser window. It can take a few minutes to create your workspace so we recommend doing it before the workshop.
Follow these steps to create your workspace:

Log in to our Coder Workspace Environment using your UCSB NetID.
Click the New Workspace button and then Coder Workspace (Summer 2026).
On the New Workspace form:

Enter a name for your workspace (or use the suggested name)
Review the usage policies and click “I understand the usage policies”.
Click Yes for “Include Visual Studio Code (Web)”
(Optional, see below) Click Yes for “Include Visual Studio Code (Desktop)”
Click the Create Workspace button
It will take a few minutes for the workspace to be created.


It may take a few minutes for the workspace to start up. Once it does, you should see a workspace dashboard. Your dashboard controls may be slightly different, depending on the options you enabled.



Coder Workspace Dashboard

title: Setup Instructions
section: Optional Setup for VS Code Desktop
text:
Optional Setup for VS Code Desktop
If you prefer to use the VS Code desktop app, rather than the browser-based version, use these instructions to set things up.

Install VS Code
We will use Visual Studio Code (VS Code) as our primary development environment. Download and install VS Code using these instructions.


Install Coder Remote Extension for VS Code
We will use the Coder Remote extension for VS Code to connect to our Coder Workspace from inside VS Code.
To install, click the green “Install” button on that page. (You should already have VS Code installed). Alternatively, you can install it through the “Extensions” panel within VS Code: search for “Coder Remote” and click “install”.


Check That Everything Works
Check that you can connect to your Coder Workspace from VS Code.

Open VS Code and click the Coder extension icon from the vertical bar on the left side of the menu. You should see a button to “Login”
Click the login button and enter https://coder.dreamlab.ucsb.edu in the text input box. Then press Enter.
Your web browser should open to a page with a button to “copy session token.” Click the “copy session token” button and close the browser window/tab.
Back in VS Code, paste the session token in the “Coder API Key” text input and press Enter.
You should see the workspace you created listed in the “My Workspaces” panel of the Coder Remote extension.

title: Multimodal Met Museum Semantic Networks
text:
Historically, analyzing visual patterns or latent themes across large-scale physical collections was a manual, slow task reserved for specialists. Traditional computer vision (like computing color histograms or edge detection) and natural language processing (like TF-IDF or keyword matching) often miss the “vibe” or deeper contextual and symbolic connections of art.
Using a multimodal Vision-Language Model (VLM) like Gemini, we can perform “semantic feature extraction” (essentially a reverse CLIP approach) to describe and tag visual elements into structured natural language concepts, and map these interconnections into an interactive network.



Query the Metropolitan Museum of Art Open Access API to fetch public domain paintings.
Download the images and store metadata locally.
Use the Gemini API with a .env file to programmatically analyze each artwork, returning structured JSON tags (dominant colors, visual subjects, emotional mood, latent research themes).
Build an interactive HTML network graph connecting artworks to their shared semantic concepts.
Document your work in a Quarto document and share your project on GitHub.





API: We will use the The Metropolitan Museum of Art Open Access API, which requires no API keys and allows searching and retrieving public-domain collections.
Gemini API Key: You must use a .env file to securely load your GEMINI_API_KEY.
Libraries:

If using Python: favor requests, python-dotenv, google-genai (or google-generativeai), and pyvis for interactive network visualization.
If using R: favor httr2, dotenv, gemini.R (or similar package), and visNetwork or networkD3 for the interactive network.






Session 1: API Fetching & Gemini Feature Extraction

Brainstorm with your agent in plan mode to handle the Met API query. Fetch details for 15-20 public-domain paintings from a specific department (e.g., “European Paintings” or “Asian Art”).
Ensure your agent writes clean, reproducible code to download images to a local folder (e.g., images/) and save basic metadata (title, artist, year, medium) in a CSV file.
Instruct your agent to write a script that securely loads the Gemini API key from .env.
Iterate over the images, sending both the image file and its metadata to Gemini. Instruct the model to return a structured JSON response extracting:

Dominant palette: 3 distinct visual colors (e.g., “warm gold”, “deep indigo”).
Visual concepts: Primary subjects/objects visible in the work.
Mood/Vibe: The overall emotional tone (e.g., “melancholic”, “serene”, “triumphant”).
Research themes: Latent themes (e.g., “social status”, “nature”, “domesticity”).

Combine Gemini’s output with the original metadata into an enriched dataset (e.g., enriched_collection.csv).

Session 2: Mapping the Semantic Web

Work with your agent to write a visualization script using the enriched dataset.
Define your network logic & edge weights: There are two great ways to model this network:

Option A: Bipartite Graph (Artworks connected to Tags): Nodes are both Artworks and Semantic Tags (colors, vibes, themes). Edges are unweighted, representing a binary match (e.g. Artwork \(A\) has tag “melancholic”).
Option B: Projected Graph (Artworks connected to Artworks): Nodes are only Artworks. An edge is drawn directly between Artwork \(A\) and Artwork \(B\). The edge weight is the number of shared semantic tags between them (e.g., if both share “warm gold”, “nature”, and “serene”, the weight is 3). Thicker lines represent stronger visual or thematic links!

Build an interactive HTML network graph where:

Nodes represent either the artworks or the extracted concepts depending on your chosen network logic above.
Edges (lines) represent connections, optionally adjusting the line thickness (weight) based on the number of shared attributes if using Option B.
Styling: Color-code artwork nodes by historical century or artistic movement, and attribute nodes by category.

Open the generated HTML and explore the interactive graph. Look for unexpected connections—such as a 17th-century oil painting and a 20th-century piece clustering together based on shared mood or latent research themes.
Include this interactive visualization in your Quarto document, add your findings and analysis, and publish the final site to GitHub Pages.

title: AI-Assisted Coding for Research
text: COVID-19 fatality rates: https://dreamlab-public.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/ocfl/model-tasks/v1/content/data/USCounties_cases.csv Download the csv and give me a general description of the data and each column.

title: AI-Assisted coding for research
text:
This hands-on three half-day workshop covers AI-assisted coding techniques with an emphasis on research data analysis, reproducibility, and best practices of Open Science. Learners will use real-world scenarios to explore concepts and capabilities of coding agents and other LLM-based techniques. Workshop activities touch on many aspects of the research lifecycle: from data discovery and access, to visualization and publication.
This is an experimental workshop covering a very new and rapidly evolving landscape. Coding agent sandboxes and LLM API access will be provided to participants. As a prerequisite, learners should have familiarity with a programming language.
